finding: 10.1 anchors the project-wide scan at 'the directory containing the discovered `.tp/`', but `tp config --extract` is normally run when no `.tp/config.json` exists (11.4 refuses to run when it does, absent --force) and frequently when no `.tp/` directory exists at all — so for the extractor's primary use case the scan root is undefined.
suggestion: Specify the scan root for `tp config --extract` when no `.tp/` is discovered — e.g. anchor at the `.git` project root (2.2.2), or the current working directory when not inside a git repository.

finding: 'project root' is defined two ways: 2.2.2 and 4.1 define it as the directory containing `.git`, while 7.1.1 and 10.1 define it as 'the directory containing the discovered `.tp/`'. When a `.tp/` is discovered in a subdirectory below the `.git` boundary (permitted by 2.2.1, which walks up from CWD and stops at the first `.tp/`), these two definitions diverge, making the `active` relative base (7.1/7.2.2) and the project-wide scan root (10.1) ambiguous — the scan could cover only the subtree and silently miss sibling task files elsewhere in the repo.
suggestion: Pick one canonical definition of 'project root' (recommend the `.git` boundary directory from 2.2.2) and use it consistently for the active base, the escape check, and the 10.x scan root; or state explicitly that a discovered `.tp/` must sit at the `.git` boundary.

finding: The term 'project root' is defined two incompatible ways. §2.2.2 and §4.1 call the directory containing '.git' the project root, while §7.1.1 and §10.1 call 'the directory containing the discovered .tp/' the project root. §2.1.3 explicitly allows a hand-created .tp/, and §2.2.1 discovers .tp/ by walking up from the CWD and stopping at the first hit, so .tp/ can resolve in a subdirectory below the .git directory. In that case the two definitions point to different directories, and a tester cannot determine the relative base for 'active' (§7.1) or the scan root for §10.
location: 2.2 Discovery (item 2) / 7.1 Active-file pointer (item 1) / 10 Project-wide task-file discovery (item 1)
suggestion: Pick one anchor for 'project root' (either the .git boundary directory or the directory containing the discovered .tp/) and make §2.2.2, §4.1, §7.1.1 and §10.1 agree; add a test case where .tp/ lives below the .git directory.

finding: The term 'project root' is defined two incompatible ways. §2.2.2 (line 56) and §4.1 (line 128) define it as 'the directory containing .git'. §7.1.1 (line 181) and §10.1 (line 241) redefine it as 'the directory containing the discovered .tp/'. These diverge whenever a hand-created .tp/ sits in a subdirectory below the .git root — a layout §2.2.1 discovery explicitly supports (walk up from CWD, stop at the first .tp/, halting only at the .git boundary). The overload makes the `active` path base (§7.1.1), the 'escapes the project root' check (§7.2.2), and the project-wide scan root (§9.1/§10.1) ambiguous.
suggestion: Pick one canonical definition of 'project root' (the .git-containing boundary directory) and give the .tp/ location a distinct name (e.g. 'config directory'). Then state explicitly whether the `active` path base and the §10 scan root are anchored at the .git root or at the discovered .tp/ dir, and reconcile with §4.1, which always creates .tp/ at the .git root.

finding: 'starting at the project root ... recurse downward into its subdirectories, collecting every *.tasks.json' does not state whether a *.tasks.json sitting directly in the project root (not in any subdirectory) is collected. 'recurse downward into its subdirectories' can be read as excluding root-level files. Two implementations would scan different file sets, changing tp validate --project and tp config --extract results.
suggestion: State explicitly that the scan includes the project root directory itself plus all non-excluded descendants, e.g. 'collect every *.tasks.json in the project root and its subdirectories'.

finding: '--extract refuses to run on a dirty working tree (exit 4)' does not define 'dirty'. Whether untracked files count, or only modified/staged tracked files, changes the outcome on a repo that has only untracked files: `git diff --quiet` (ignores untracked) and `git status --porcelain` (includes untracked) give different answers, so two implementations disagree on whether --extract runs.
suggestion: Define 'dirty' precisely — e.g. 'any output from git status --porcelain, including untracked files' — so the refusal is deterministically testable.

finding: Neither 9 nor 10 defines what the project-wide scan does when a discovered `*.tasks.json` is unreadable or not valid JSON: does `tp validate --project` / `tp config --extract` abort the whole run, or skip that file with a warning and continue? This matters most for --extract, where '**every** discovered task file sets that field with an identical value' (11.3) cannot be evaluated safely if one file cannot be parsed.
location: 9 Drift / deviation reporting / 10 Project-wide task-file discovery
suggestion: State the per-file failure policy for the project-wide scan — e.g. skip-with-warning for `tp validate --project`, but abort with a non-zero exit for `tp config --extract` since a partial hoist would be unsafe.

finding: 11 does not define what `tp config --extract` does when no workflow field qualifies for hoisting (the discovered task files agree on nothing, so the computed common policy is empty): does it still create `.tp/config.json` with an empty `workflow: {}` block, or skip creation and report 'nothing to hoist'? The --dry-run/plan wording assumes a non-empty plan.
suggestion: State the empty-common-policy outcome — recommend skipping `.tp/config.json` creation and reporting that no field was common, exiting 0.

finding: Type-mismatch handling is enumerated only for 'active' (non-string) and individual 'defaults' entries (non-boolean). Behavior when the container values themselves have the wrong type is unspecified: e.g. {"workflow": 42} or {"workflow": []} (workflow not an object), or {"defaults": "x"} (defaults not an object). §2.3.6's malformed rule covers only a non-object TOP-LEVEL value, so these cases match no rule (warning? exit 3? ignore?).
suggestion: State that a 'workflow' or 'defaults' value that is not a JSON object is a validation warning treated as unset (or, if intended, malformed → exit 3). Add explicit fixtures for each.

finding: The drift report line format is given only for scalar fields ('review_max_rounds=0 overrides project default 8'). 'checks' is a workflow field that can deviate (§9.3 even defines its equality), but the report-line format for a checks-array deviation (how the override array and the project array are rendered) is unspecified, so a tester cannot assert the exact drift output for a checks difference.
suggestion: Specify the drift line format for array-valued fields like 'checks' (or state it is reported by count/summary) with a concrete example.

finding: The 'source' enum is cli/env/override/project/local/default, but §6.2 maps 'active' only to the 'local' case. Per §7.1, 'active' can also be supplied by --file, TP_FILE, legacy .tp-active, or auto-detect. There is no enum value for legacy '.tp-active' or 'auto-detect', and the source reported when active comes from --file or TP_FILE is unstated, so active.source in 'tp config --resolved' is undefined for four of the five discovery-chain positions.
suggestion: Map every §7.1 discovery-chain position to a 'source' value (e.g. cli for --file, env for TP_FILE, local for .tp/local.json:active, plus values for legacy '.tp-active' and 'auto-detect') with an example of a non-local active source.

finding: §11.3 hoists 'a workflow field' and then 'removes each hoisted field from every task file's workflow block'. It is unspecified whether 'quality_gate' — a workflow field per §2.3.1 but read-only per-task — participates in --extract. If hoisted, --extract removes a per-task read-only field (a mutation the author cannot make via tp set); if excluded, that exception is undocumented. A tester cannot predict whether quality_gate (or checks) is extracted.
suggestion: State explicitly whether --extract considers 'quality_gate' and 'checks' for hoisting/removal; if any workflow field is excluded, list it.

finding: `tp config --extract --force` 'overwrites that file's workflow block with the newly computed common policy'. The common policy contains only fields on which every discovered task file agrees (§11.3), so a full-block overwrite silently drops any pre-existing project-level workflow field in .tp/config.json that extraction did not reproduce — e.g. a hand-set review_max_rounds=8 that the task files do not uniformly declare. This is unannounced loss of committed project policy.
suggestion: Either merge the extracted common policy into the existing workflow block (adding/updating only hoisted fields), or require --force to first report which existing project fields will be removed; document whichever behavior is chosen.

finding: Round-3 edits introduced two conflicting definitions of "project root". §2.2.2 states "The directory containing `.git` is called the project root" (and §4.1 repeats "the project root — the boundary directory that contains `.git`"), but §7.1.1 and §10.1 define "the project root (the directory containing the discovered `.tp/`)". Because §2.2.1's upward walk stops at the first `.tp/` hit and may find it in a subdirectory below the `.git` boundary, the two definitions diverge. This makes the §10 recursion start directory, the §7.1 base for the relative `active` path, and the §7.2.2 "escapes the project root" boundary check ambiguous whenever `.tp/` is not located at the git root.
location: §2.2 Discovery (line 56) vs §7.1 Active-file pointer (line 181-182) / §10 Project-wide task-file discovery (line 241)
suggestion: Use one canonical term. Either rename the git-boundary directory in §2.2.2/§4.1 to "repository boundary" and reserve "project root" exclusively for the directory containing the discovered `.tp/` (used in §7.1/§7.2/§10), or state explicitly that `.tp/` always lives at the git root so the two coincide and reconcile §2.2.1's upward walk with that constraint.

finding: 8.2 introduces paired boolean flags (`--compact`/`--no-compact`, `--quiet`/`--no-quiet`, `--color`/`--no-color`) but does not define behavior when a flag and its negation are both passed in one invocation (e.g. `--compact --no-compact`). 'An explicit CLI flag always wins' does not disambiguate two conflicting explicit flags.
suggestion: Specify a rule for conflicting paired flags — e.g. last-one-wins, or reject with exit 2.

finding: The precedence table lists '--file' (rank 1) and 'TP_FILE (active)' (rank 2) as sources in the workflow ladder, but note 3 says 'active is resolved by the discovery chain in §7.1, not by this workflow ladder'. Active resolution is thus specified in two places; a tester reading only the table would build the wrong resolver, since the table has no rank for .tp/local.json:active, legacy .tp-active, or auto-detect.
suggestion: Either remove the active-related entries (--file, 'TP_FILE (active)') from the §3.1 table and resolve active solely in §7.1, or add a table note pointing to §7.1 for the full active chain.
